topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air hung thick and still around the clearing. I stopped dead, my breath catching in my throat as my eyes traced the raw, jagged stump where the oak used to stand. It was only a few years ago, a sapling I’d nurtured with painstaking care, a tiny promise of shade and steadfastness. Now, it was just splintered wood, a stark, ugly reminder of absence. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles bleached white. I took a hesitant step forward, my boots crunching on the dry leaves, and then another. A low, ragged sound escaped my chest, a sound that felt too large for my frame. I sank to my knees, resting my forehead against the rough bark of the remaining trunk, the texture cold against my skin. The silence of the woods seemed to press in, heavy and judgmental.

[story 2]

I found it by accident while walking the familiar path. The clearing was strangely barren where the giant maple had stood. It wasn't just missing; it was obliterated, the base ripped away as if by a brutal, swift violence. A sudden, sharp ache bloomed behind my ribs, a deep, unsettling hollowness. I stood there for a long moment, just watching the debris scattered across the moss. My jaw tightened, and I felt a strange tremor run through my legs. I didn't move to touch anything; the sight was too overwhelming. Slowly, I turned away, my shoulders slumping inward as if trying to fold myself into something smaller and less visible. Every instinct screamed at me to run, but my feet remained rooted, heavy and unresponsive to the command.

[story 3]

It took me nearly an hour to reach the edge of the devastation. When I finally stood in the center of the clearing, the reality hit with a physical jolt. The tree—my tree—was gone, reduced to stumps that looked like wounds in the earth. A sudden, hot sting welled up in my eyes, blurring the edges of the scene. I walked a slow circle around the perimeter, my gaze snagging on the empty space where years of quiet growth had been. My voice, when I finally managed to speak, was thin and reedy, barely a whisper. "No," I managed, the sound catching somewhere in my throat. I shook my head once, a small, jerky motion, and then I turned abruptly, walking away without looking back, the weight of the moment pressing down on my chest like a physical stone.
